name: hig-components-content
description: Comprehensive Apple Human Interface Guidelines for content display components including charts, collections, images, web views, color wells, and more. Provides best practices for implementing accessible, platform-adaptive UI components in Apple applications.
capabilities: Provides component selection guidance for charts, collections, image views, web views, color wells, activity views, and lockups
Explains accessibility requirements for each content component type including VoiceOver support and audio graphs
Documents platform-specific behavior differences across iOS, iPadOS, macOS, tvOS, watchOS, and visionOS
Offers configuration guidance for Swift Charts, UICollectionView, WKWebView, and other system components
Includes best practices for performance optimization, empty states, and visual hierarchy
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Documentation-only skill containing Apple Human Interface Guidelines in Markdown format. No executable code, no runtime behavior, and no security risks. All static analysis findings are false positives from Markdown content (inline code blocks, documentation URLs, and legitimate UI terminology).
valueStatement: Struggling to implement charts, collections, or content displays that feel native to Apple platforms? This skill provides authoritative Human Interface Guidelines to help you build accessible, platform-adaptive components that follow Apple's design principles.
userTitle: Design Apple UI Components with Confidence
limitations: Reference documentation only - does not generate executable code or implementations
Focuses on Apple platforms - not applicable to Android, web, or cross-platform development
Requires familiarity with Swift, SwiftUI, or UIKit frameworks for practical application
Supplements but does not replace official Apple Developer documentation

useCases.targetUser: iOS Developer Building a Data Dashboard
useCases.title: iOS Developer Building a Data Dashboard
useCases.description: An iOS developer needs to display fitness tracking data in charts. This skill helps them choose between bar and line marks, configure axis ranges, and implement audio graph accessibility.

useCases.title: UI/UX Designer Creating tvOS Interface
useCases.description: A designer is building a media browsing app for Apple TV. This skill provides guidance on lockup components, focus effects, and shelf layouts specific to tvOS.

useCases.title: Cross-Platform Developer Ensuring Accessibility
useCases.description: A developer needs to ensure their collection view works properly with VoiceOver and Switch Control across all Apple platforms. This skill provides accessibility requirements and testing guidance.

promptTemplates.title: Component Selection
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to display [content type] in my [platform] app. What component should I use and why?
promptTemplates.scenario: User needs help choosing the right UI component for their content display needs

promptTemplates.title: Accessibility Implementation
promptTemplates.prompt: How do I make [component] accessible for VoiceOver users? What labels and descriptions do I need?
promptTemplates.scenario: User needs guidance on implementing accessibility for a specific component

promptTemplates.title: Platform-Specific Behavior
promptTemplates.prompt: How does [component] behave differently on [platform] compared to [other platform]? What adaptations do I need to make?
promptTemplates.scenario: User wants to understand component differences across Apple platforms

promptTemplates.title: Configuration and Best Practices
promptTemplates.prompt: What are the key properties I should configure for [component]? What are the performance and accessibility best practices?
promptTemplates.scenario: User needs detailed configuration guidance and best practices for a component

bestPractices: Always check for existing design context in `.claude/apple-design-context.md` before asking questions - this skill builds on foundational color, typography, and accessibility guidelines
Consider platform-specific behavior early - a collection that works on iOS may need significant adaptation for tvOS (lockups) or visionOS (depth and hover effects)
Design accessibility from the start - charts need audio graph support, images need alt text, and all components need proper VoiceOver labels
antiPatterns: Don't choose a component based solely on visual appearance - always consider accessibility requirements, platform conventions, and data characteristics
Avoid implementing custom components from scratch when system components (Swift Charts, UICollectionView, WKWebView) provide built-in accessibility and platform adaptation
Don't skip empty states - blank screens confuse users; provide meaningful guidance on how to populate empty collections or data displays

faq.question: What's the difference between a collection view and a table view?
faq.answer: Collection views support complex grid and custom layouts using compositional layout, while table views are limited to single-column lists. Use collections for grids, grids-within-grids, and multi-column layouts. Use tables for simple lists. Both support diffable data sources and lazy loading for performance.

faq.question: Should I use WKWebView or SFSafariViewController?
faq.answer: Use WKWebView when you need to display web content inline within your app's interface and control the navigation experience. Use SFSafariViewController for external web browsing - it provides a familiar Safari interface with reading mode, auto-fill, and sharing features.

faq.question: How do I choose between bar, line, and point marks in charts?
faq.answer: Use bar marks to compare values across categories or show parts of a whole. Use line marks to show trends over time when values represent continuous change. Use point marks to depict individual distinct values and show relationships between two data properties.

faq.question: What's a lockup and when should I use one?
faq.answer: A lockup is a content card that combines an image with text, typically used for media browsing. Lockups are primarily used on tvOS where they appear as cards with parallax effects. On other platforms, you can adapt the lockup pattern for similar card-based layouts. Use lockups when displaying browsable media content like movies, TV shows, or products.

faq.question: Do I need to support all Apple platforms mentioned?
faq.answer: No - support only the platforms your app targets. However, be aware that if you later expand to other platforms, component behavior may differ significantly. For example, a collection optimized for iOS touch interaction needs substantial changes for tvOS focus navigation or visionOS depth effects.

faq.question: What's the minimum accessibility I need to implement for charts?
faq.answer: Every chart needs accessibility labels describing its purpose and structure. Provide individual labels for interactive marks or meaningful groups of marks. Support Audio Graphs to give VoiceOver users an auditory representation of data trends. Hide visible axis labels from assistive technologies since VoiceOver reports values through labels. See charts.md for complete requirements.
